children.name: Setophaga
children.description: The typical wood-warblers — a large and diverse genus of small, insectivorous passerines that forms the core of the Parulidae. Males in breeding plumage display bold patterns of yellow, black, white, blue, and chestnut; females and non-breeding birds are more subdued. They are among the most sought-after birds by North American birders during spring migration, when males sing from treetops in brilliant breeding colours.

children.name: Setophaga ruticilla
children.description: A hyperactive warbler that constantly fans its colourful tail and droops its wings, flashing orange and black patches to flush insects from foliage. The male is black with vivid orange patches on the tail, wings, and sides; the female is grey with yellow patches. It breeds across much of North America and winters in the Caribbean, Central America, and northern South America.

children.name: Setophaga petechia
children.description: The most widespread Setophaga warbler, with 43 subspecies across the Americas. The male is brilliant yellow with fine chestnut streaks on the breast and a sweet, whistled 'sweet-sweet-sweet-I'm-so-sweet' song. It inhabits riparian thickets, willow swamps, and gardens across North America, and mangroves in the Caribbean and Central America, where resident subspecies are found year-round.

children.name: Setophaga coronata
children.description: One of the most abundant and widespread North American wood-warblers, distinguished by its bright yellow rump and yellow patches on the sides. It is uniquely adapted among warblers to digest waxy fruits, allowing it to winter farther north and survive on bayberries when insects are scarce. It is often the first warbler to arrive in spring and the last to leave in autumn.

children.name: Setophaga caerulescens
children.description: A strikingly dimorphic warbler: the male is deep blue above with a black face, throat, and flanks contrasting with a white belly; the female is plain olive-brown with a distinctive white wing patch. It breeds in the understory of mature deciduous and mixed forests in eastern North America and winters in Caribbean forests, especially in Cuba and Jamaica.

children.name: Setophaga fusca
children.description: Arguably the most brilliantly coloured of all North American warblers: the male has a flaming orange throat, neck, and crown set against a black head and cheek, with white wing patches. It breeds in mature coniferous and mixed forests of northeastern North America, favouring large trees for its high canopy foraging. Its thin, wiry, high-pitched song seems to ascend past the range of human hearing.

children.name: Setophaga americana
children.description: A small, dainty warbler with a blue-grey head and back, two bold white wing bars, and a yellow patch on the back. The male has a chestnut band across the yellow breast. It breeds in mature forests rich in epiphytic moss and lichen, particularly Usnea and Tillandsia, in which it nests. Its buzzy, rising trill that ends with an abrupt note is a distinctive sound of eastern North American woodlands.

children.name: Setophaga magnolia
children.description: A striking warbler with a bold pattern: yellow underparts heavily streaked with black, a white supercilium, and a distinctive white band across the tail visible in flight. The male has a black mask bordered by yellow. It breeds in young coniferous forests across Canada and the northeastern United States and winters in the Caribbean and Central America. Its emphatic 'weeta-weeta-wichee' song is easily recognised.

children.name: Setophaga striata
children.description: The Blackpoll Warbler is a black-and-white streaked warbler with a solid black cap and white cheeks. It breeds in the boreal forests of Canada and Alaska, undertaking one of the longest migrations of any North American warbler, including a nonstop transoceanic flight from the northeastern United States to South America.

children.name: Setophaga virens
children.description: The Black-throated Green Warbler has a yellow face, olive-green back, and black throat and breast, with white wing bars. It breeds in coniferous and mixed forests across Canada and the eastern United States, wintering in Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Setophaga townsendi
children.description: Townsend's Warbler has a yellow face with black cheek patches and crown, olive-green back, and yellow underparts with black streaking. It breeds in mature coniferous forests of the Pacific Northwest and winters in woodlands of Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Setophaga occidentalis
children.description: The Hermit Warbler has a bright yellow head, grey upperparts, white underparts, and a distinctive dark throat patch. It breeds in montane coniferous forests of the Pacific coast and winters in Central America.

children.name: Setophaga graciae
children.description: Grace's Warbler has a yellow throat, breast, and supercilium, grey upperparts, white wing bars, and streaked flanks. It breeds in pine-oak woodlands of the southwestern United States and Mexico, gleaning insects from pine needles in the canopy.

children.name: Setophaga dominica
children.description: The Yellow-throated Warbler has a bright yellow throat and breast, grey upperparts, white wing bars, and a white belly with black streaks. It breeds in bottomland hardwood forests and pine plantations of the southeastern United States, foraging deliberately along branches.

children.name: Setophaga palmarum
children.description: The Palm Warbler is a brownish-olive warbler with a yellow throat and undertail, chestnut cap, and constant tail-bobbing behaviour. It breeds in boreal bogs and muskegs across Canada and winters in the southeastern United States and the Caribbean.

children.name: Setophaga discolor
children.description: The Prairie Warbler has a yellow throat and underparts with black streaks on the flanks, olive-green upperparts, and a distinctive dark facial pattern. It inhabits early-successional scrub, pine barrens, and mangroves in eastern North America. Its ascending, buzzy song is a characteristic sound of overgrown fields and coastal thickets.

children.name: Setophaga castanea
children.description: The Bay-breasted Warbler has a rich chestnut crown, throat, and flanks in breeding males, with a buffy face and black mask. It breeds in the boreal forests of Canada, where its population fluctuates with spruce budworm outbreaks. It winters in the forests of Panama and northern South America.

children.name: Setophaga cerulea
children.description: The Cerulean Warbler is a brilliant sky-blue warbler with white underparts and a narrow black breast band. It requires large tracts of mature deciduous forest for breeding in the eastern United States and has declined significantly. It winters in the eastern slopes of the Andes in South America.

children.name: Setophaga pensylvanica
children.description: The Chestnut-sided Warbler is a strikingly patterned warbler with a yellow crown, black face markings, white underparts, and rich chestnut flanks. It breeds in early-successional forests and shrubby fields across eastern North America. Its exuberant song is a loud, clear 'pleased-pleased-pleased-to-meet-you'.

children.name: Setophaga tigrina
children.description: The Cape May Warbler has a yellow belly with black streaks, a chestnut cheek patch, and a distinctive curved bill adapted for feeding on nectar. It breeds in the boreal spruce forests of Canada and winters in the Caribbean, where it visits flowers for nectar.

children.name: Setophaga pinus
children.description: The Pine Warbler is a plain olive-yellow warbler with white wing bars and a yellow throat and breast, closely associated with pine forests across eastern North America. It is unique among warblers in regularly eating seeds and suet at feeders. Its musical, trilling song is a common sound in pine woodlands.

children.name: Setophaga kirtlandii
children.description: Kirtland's Warbler is a large, grey-and-yellow warbler with a broken white eye-ring and a habit of bobbing its tail. It has one of the most restricted breeding ranges of any North American bird, nesting only in young jack pine forests of Michigan. Intensive conservation efforts have brought it back from fewer than 200 pairs to several thousand.

children.name: Setophaga adelaidae
children.description: Adelaide's Warbler is a grey-and-yellow warbler endemic to Puerto Rico, with a yellow throat and underparts, grey upperparts, and white wing bars. It inhabits a variety of forest types across the island, foraging actively for insects. Its distinctive song is a series of clear whistles.

children.name: Setophaga subita
children.description: The Barbuda Warbler is a yellow-throated warbler with a grey head and back, endemic to the island of Barbuda in the Lesser Antilles. It inhabits scrub and forest across the island. It is classified as Vulnerable due to its restricted range and threats from hurricanes and habitat degradation.

children.name: Setophaga delicata
children.description: The St. Lucia Warbler is a grey-and-yellow warbler endemic to the island of Saint Lucia in the Lesser Antilles. It has a grey head, yellow throat and breast, and white wing bars. It inhabits forests and woodlands across the island.

children.name: Setophaga pharetra
children.description: The Arrowhead Warbler is a streaked grey-and-white warbler endemic to Jamaica, with distinctive dark arrow-shaped markings on the breast and flanks. It inhabits montane forests and forages actively in the canopy. Its song is a thin, high-pitched trill.

children.name: Setophaga plumbea
children.description: The Plumbeous Warbler is a uniform grey warbler with a white throat and belly, endemic to the islands of Dominica and Guadeloupe in the Lesser Antilles. It inhabits a variety of forest types, foraging for insects in the canopy.

children.name: Setophaga angelae
children.description: The Elfin Woods Warbler is a striking black-and-white warbler with a white throat, black mask, and white spots on the wings, endemic to Puerto Rico. It is found only in the highest elevation elfin cloud forests of the Caribbean National Forest. It is classified as Endangered due to its extremely restricted range.

children.name: Setophaga chrysoparia
children.description: The Golden-cheeked Warbler has a bright yellow face with a black crown and throat, black upperparts, and white underparts with black streaks. It is endemic to central Texas, breeding only in mature Ashe juniper-oak woodlands. It is listed as Endangered due to habitat loss on its restricted breeding grounds.

children.name: Setophaga pityophila
children.description: The Olive-capped Warbler has an olive-green cap, yellow face, white eye-ring, and grey upperparts with white wing bars, found in Cuba and the Bahamas. It inhabits pine forests and forages for insects in the canopy.

children.name: Setophaga flavescens
children.description: The Bahama Warbler has a yellow throat and breast, olive-green upperparts, and a distinctive white stripe over the eye, endemic to the Bahama archipelago. It inhabits pine forests and broadleaf woodlands, foraging for insects in the mid-storey.

children.name: Setophaga vitellina
children.description: The Vitelline Warbler has bright yellow underparts, olive-green upperparts, and a yellow supercilium, endemic to the Cayman Islands and the Swan Islands of Honduras. It inhabits a variety of wooded habitats and forages actively for insects.

children.name: Setophaga nigrescens
children.description: The Black-throated Grey Warbler has a bold black-and-white striped head, grey back, black throat, and white underparts with black streaks on the flanks. It breeds in dry montane forests of western North America and winters in western Mexico.

children.name: Setophaga pitiayumi
children.description: The Tropical Parula is a small blue-grey warbler with yellow underparts and a green patch on the back, found from southern Texas through Central and South America. It inhabits forests and woodlands.

children.name: Setophaga batesii
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with distinctive white eye ring and a short tail. Known for its melodious song, this species can be found in dense evergreen forests along the Pacific Northwest coast.

children.name: Geothlypis flavida
children.description: A small, greenish warbler with a bright yellow throat and breast. Known for its loud, high-pitched calls during the breeding season, this species is found in deciduous forests and woodlands.

children.name: Protonotaria grisea
children.description: A small, olive-brown warbler with a strikingly white throat and underparts. Known for its distinctive hooting song, this species is found in wooded swamps and wetlands of the southeastern United States.

children.name: Seiurus ohiensis
children.description: A small, brown warbler with a distinctive white band across the breast and yellow flanks. Known for its steady hopping on the ground in search of insect prey, this species is found along streams and rivers in eastern North America.

children.name: Mniotilta vellei
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a bright white throat and breast. Known for its loud, raspy calls during the breeding season, this species is found in forests along the Atlantic coast.

children.name: Parkesia albifrons
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a yellow breast and belly. Known for its distinctive call that sounds like the bark of an old oak tree, this species is found in deciduous forests.

children.name: Cardellina leucophrys
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a bright white throat and breast. Known for its loud, buzzy song during the breeding season, this species is found in coastal wetlands.

children.name: Leiothlypis brevicauda
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a white eyebrow and breast. Known for its high-pitched trills during the breeding season, this species is found in open woodlands.

children.name: Oporornis dominica
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a white throat and breast. Known for its loud, whistling song during the breeding season, this species is found in coastal forests.

children.name: Vermivora cinerea
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a black throat and breast. Known for its distinctive call that sounds like “cheer cheer,” this species is found in deciduous forests.

children.name: Myioborus lucernarius
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a white throat and breast. Known for its melodious song during the breeding season, this species is found in coastal forests of South America.

children.name: Basileuterus rufogularis
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a rufous throat and breast. Known for its distinctive call that sounds like “chip chip,” this species is found in tropical forests.

children.name: Myiothlypis fulvogularis
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a yellow throat and breast. Known for its melodious song during the breeding season, this species is found in coastal forests of South America.

children.name: Oreothlypis nigriceps
children.description: A small, olive-green warbler with a black throat and breast. Known for its loud, raspy calls during the breeding season, this species is found in coastal forests.

children.name: Geothlypis
children.description: The yellowthroats — a genus of stocky, skulking warblers of dense, wet vegetation. Males are distinguished by a bold black mask across the face, bordered above by a white or grey band. They are more often heard than seen, delivering a loud, repetitive 'wichity-wichity-wichity' song from the depths of cattail marshes and brushy swamps.

children.name: Geothlypis trichas
children.description: One of the most widespread and familiar warblers in North America, found in almost every marsh, swamp, and brushy field from Alaska to Mexico. The male is unmistakable with its bold black bandit mask bordered by white. Its cheerful 'wichity-wichity-wichity' song is a hallmark of wetland habitats, and it skulks in dense vegetation, popping up only briefly to scold observers.

children.name: Geothlypis formosa
children.description: The Kentucky Warbler is a striking yellow-and-black warbler with a bold black mask, yellow spectacles, and olive-green upperparts. It skulks in the understory of mature deciduous forests of the southeastern United States and winters in Central and northern South America.

children.name: Geothlypis philadelphia
children.description: The Mourning Warbler has a grey hood, black throat patch, and bright yellow underparts, with no eye-ring or supercilium. It breeds in dense second-growth thickets of boreal Canada and winters in Central and South America.

children.name: Geothlypis tolmiei
children.description: MacGillivray's Warbler has a grey hood, broken white eye-rings, and bright yellow underparts. It breeds in dense brushy thickets of western North America and winters in Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Geothlypis beldingi
children.description: Belding's Yellowthroat is a bright yellow warbler with a black mask in males, endemic to the Baja California Peninsula of Mexico. It inhabits freshwater marshes and reed beds along the Pacific coast. It is classified as Endangered due to habitat loss and water diversion.

children.name: Geothlypis flavovelata
children.description: The Altamira Yellowthroat is a striking yellow warbler with a black mask bordered by yellow, found only in the freshwater marshes of the Tamaulipas coast in northeastern Mexico. It is classified as Endangered due to habitat destruction for agriculture and urban development.

children.name: Geothlypis rostrata
children.description: The Bahama Yellowthroat has a black mask, yellow throat and breast, olive-green upperparts, and a relatively large bill, found in the Bahama archipelago. It inhabits marshes, wetlands, and damp scrub. Some authorities split it into multiple species across the islands.

children.name: Geothlypis speciosa
children.description: The Black-polled Yellowthroat has a yellow throat and breast, olive upperparts, and the male has a black crown and mask. It is endemic to the highlands of central Mexico, inhabiting freshwater marshes and tule beds. It is classified as Endangered due to wetland drainage.

children.name: Geothlypis aequinoctialis
children.description: The Masked Yellowthroat has a black mask in males, yellow throat and breast, and olive-green upperparts, found across much of South America east of the Andes. It inhabits marshes, wet grasslands, and rice fields, foraging low in dense vegetation.

children.name: Geothlypis poliocephala
children.description: The Grey-crowned Yellowthroat has a grey crown, yellow throat and underparts, and olive-green upperparts, found from Mexico to Panama. Unlike most yellowthroats, males lack a black mask. It inhabits scrubby fields, forest edges, and secondary growth.

children.name: Geothlypis semiflava
children.description: The Slate-throated Yellowthroat has a slate-grey hood, bright yellow underparts, and olive-green upperparts, found in the Pacific lowlands of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. It inhabits wet scrub, bamboo thickets, and forest edges. The male lacks the black mask typical of other yellowthroats.

children.name: Protonotaria
children.description: The prothonotary warbler — a monotypic genus of cavity-nesting warbler named for the golden-yellow robes of papal notaries. It is one of only two Parulidae species that nest in tree cavities, and the only eastern North American warbler to do so. Its brilliant colouration and gentle song make it one of the most prized species among birders.

children.name: Protonotaria citrea
children.description: A stunning golden-yellow warbler with blue-grey wings and a bright orange-yellow head and breast. It is one of only two cavity-nesting warblers in North America, readily using nest boxes placed in swamps and flooded woodlands. It breeds in bottomland hardwood forests of the southeastern United States and winters in mangroves along the Caribbean coasts of Central and South America.

children.name: Seiurus
children.description: The ovenbird — a monotypic genus of large, terrestrial warbler named for its distinctive domed nest, which resembles a dutch oven. It is one of the few warblers that forages entirely on the forest floor, walking deliberately in search of invertebrates. Its loud, ringing 'teacher-TEACHER-TEACHER' song is one of the most powerful and distinctive sounds of eastern North American forests.

children.name: Seiurus aurocapilla
children.description: A thrush-like warbler of the forest floor, with an olive-brown back, heavily streaked white breast, and a distinctive orange crown bordered by black stripes. It builds a remarkable domed nest of leaves and grasses on the ground with a side entrance, resembling a Dutch oven. Its song, a series of emphatic 'teacher' notes increasing in volume, carries remarkably far through the woodland understory.

children.name: Mniotilta
children.description: The black-and-white warbler — a distinctive monotypic genus with a unique foraging behaviour that sets it apart from all other Parulidae. It creeps along tree trunks and branches like a nuthatch, probing bark crevices for hidden insects. Its black-and-white streaked plumage resembles that of a tiny woodpecker, and its squeaky, wheel-like song is a familiar sound of North American woodlands.

children.name: Mniotilta varia
children.description: The only warbler in North America that forages in the manner of a nuthatch or creeper, investigating bark crevices on trunks and large branches for hidden insects. Its bold black-and-white longitudinal streaking is unique among North American warblers. Its thin, squeaky 'wee-see-wee-see-wee-see' song sounds like a slowly turning wheel and is a common sound in mature eastern deciduous forests.

children.name: Parkesia
children.description: The waterthrushes — a small genus of large, terrestrial warblers restricted to wet habitats. They resemble thrushes in build and behaviour, with heavily streaked underparts and a habit of bobbing their rear body. They walk on the ground along streams and in swamps, flipping dead leaves in search of insects, and are among the most secretive of North American warblers despite their size.

children.name: Parkesia noveboracensis
children.description: A large, thrush-like warbler with heavily streaked yellowish underparts and a prominent white supercilium. It bobs its tail constantly as it walks along the edges of streams, swamps, and beaver ponds in the boreal forests of North America. Its loud, ringing 'sweet-sweet-sweet' song accelerates and descends in pitch, echoing through northern wetlands. It winters in mangroves and swamp forests from Mexico to the Amazon.

children.name: Cardellina
children.description: A small genus of colourful warblers with predominantly red or yellow plumage, including the redstarts and the Canada Warbler. They are active, acrobatic foragers of montane and boreal forests across the Americas.

children.name: Cardellina canadensis
children.description: The Canada Warbler is a striking blue-grey warbler with bright yellow underparts and a distinctive 'necklace' of black spots across the breast. It breeds in moist mixed forests of boreal Canada and the northeastern United States and winters in the Andes of South America.

children.name: Cardellina pusilla
children.description: Wilson's Warbler is a small, bright yellow warbler with a distinctive black cap in males. It breeds in dense willow and alder thickets near water across northern North America and winters from Mexico to Central America.

children.name: Cardellina rubrifrons
children.description: The Red-faced Warbler has a brilliant red face and throat, black crown, grey upperparts, and white underparts. It breeds in montane pine-oak forests of the southwestern United States and Mexico, nesting on the ground.

children.name: Leiothlypis
children.description: A genus of small, plain-faced warblers with thin pointed bills and predominantly olive or grey upperparts. They forage deliberately in trees and shrubs, and are among the most common Neotropical migrants in North America.

children.name: Leiothlypis peregrina
children.description: The Tennessee Warbler is a plain olive-green warbler with white underparts, a white supercilium, and a thin pointed bill. It breeds in boreal forests of Canada and winters in Central and South America, where it feeds extensively on nectar and insects.

children.name: Leiothlypis celata
children.description: The Orange-crowned Warbler is a drab olive-green warbler with faint streaking on the underparts and a concealed orange crown patch. It is one of the hardiest warblers, wintering farther north than most and arriving early on its breeding grounds across northern North America.

children.name: Leiothlypis ruficapilla
children.description: The Nashville Warbler has a grey head, white eye-ring, yellow underparts, and olive-green upperparts. It breeds in open woodlands and bogs across Canada and the northern United States and winters in Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Leiothlypis virginiae
children.description: Virginia's Warbler is a small, grey warbler with a yellow rump and breast, a white eye-ring, and a chestnut crown patch. It breeds on dry, scrubby mountain slopes of the southwestern United States and winters in western Mexico.

children.name: Leiothlypis crissalis
children.description: The Colima Warbler is a grey warbler with a yellow rump, buff-coloured undertail coverts, and a distinctive pale eye-ring. It breeds in remote pine-oak forests of the Sierra Madre Occidental in Mexico, barely reaching into the Chisos Mountains of Texas. It is one of the least studied North American warblers.

children.name: Leiothlypis luciae
children.description: Lucy's Warbler is a small, pale grey warbler with a white eye-ring, a cinnamon rump, and pale underparts. It is the only warbler that nests in cavities in the southwestern deserts of the United States, using abandoned woodpecker holes in mesquite and saguaro. Its song is a high-pitched trill.

children.name: Oporornis
children.description: A monotypic genus of secretive, skulking warbler with a preference for wet boreal forests and bogs. It is more often heard than seen, delivering its distinctive song from deep within dense spruce and tamarack stands.

children.name: Oporornis agilis
children.description: The Connecticut Warbler is a large, olive-brown warbler with a grey hood, bold white eye-ring, and yellow underparts. It is a secretive bird of boreal bogs and muskegs, more often identified by its loud, ringing song than by sight.

children.name: Vermivora
children.description: A small genus of brightly coloured warblers with bold wing patterns and relatively large bills. They are among the most sought-after warblers by birders due to their striking beauty and the conservation concern surrounding some species.

children.name: Vermivora cyanoptera
children.description: The Blue-winged Warbler is a bright yellow warbler with blue-grey wings marked by two white wing bars and a bold black line through the eye. It breeds in early-successional habitats across eastern North America and winters in Central America.

children.name: Vermivora chrysoptera
children.description: The Golden-winged Warbler is a stunning warbler with a golden crown and wing patch, black throat and cheek, and grey body. It breeds in early-successional habitats of the Great Lakes region and Appalachians, and is one of the most rapidly declining songbirds in North America.

children.name: Myioborus
children.description: The whitestarts or redstarts — a genus of brightly coloured warblers with vivid red, yellow, and black plumage and distinctive white outer tail feathers that are constantly fanned. They are active, acrobatic foragers of montane forests from the southwestern United States through the Andes.

children.name: Myioborus pictus
children.description: The Painted Redstart is a striking black warbler with a vivid red belly and breast, white wing patches, and white outer tail feathers constantly fanned. It inhabits oak and pine-oak woodlands of the southwestern United States and Mexico.

children.name: Myioborus miniatus
children.description: The Slate-throated Redstart has a slate-grey head and throat, bright yellow underparts, and chestnut crown. It is a common inhabitant of montane forests from Mexico through Central America to the Andes of South America.

children.name: Myioborus ornatus
children.description: The Golden-fronted Redstart has a bright golden-yellow forehead and crown, black mask, yellow underparts, and grey upperparts. It is found in montane cloud forests of Colombia, Ecuador, and northern Peru.

children.name: Myioborus albifrons
children.description: The White-fronted Redstart has a white forehead, black mask, bright yellow underparts, and grey upperparts, found in the high-altitude cloud forests of the Venezuelan Andes. It constantly fans its white outer tail feathers.

children.name: Myioborus torquatus
children.description: The Collared Redstart has a bright yellow face and underparts, a distinctive chestnut collar across the nape, and grey upperparts, found in the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama. It is an active, acrobatic forager in montane cloud forests.

children.name: Myioborus flavivertex
children.description: The Yellow-crowned Redstart has a bright yellow crown, black mask, yellow underparts, and olive-grey upperparts, found in the montane forests of the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta in Colombia. It is an active forager that fans its white outer tail feathers constantly.

children.name: Myioborus albifacies
children.description: The White-faced Redstart is a distinctive redstart with a white face, black cap, yellow underparts, and grey upperparts, found in the cloud forests of the Venezuelan Coastal Range. Its white outer tail feathers are constantly fanned during foraging.

children.name: Myioborus cardonai
children.description: The Saffron-breasted Redstart has a grey head, bright yellow underparts, and a saffron-yellow breast, found in the tepui region of southern Venezuela and adjacent Brazil. It inhabits montane forests and forest edges.

children.name: Myioborus brunniceps
children.description: The Fulvous-crowned Redstart has a brownish cap, bright yellow underparts, grey upperparts, and white outer tail feathers, found in the Andes of Bolivia and northwestern Argentina. It is a common inhabitant of montane forests and scrub.

children.name: Myioborus castaneocapillus
children.description: The Tepui Redstart has a chestnut cap, black mask, yellow underparts, and grey upperparts, found in the tepui highlands of southern Venezuela and western Guyana. It inhabits montane forests and is often seen in pairs.

children.name: Myioborus melanocephalus
children.description: The Spectacled Redstart has a black head and throat, yellow underparts, and olive upperparts with prominent white spectacles around the eyes, found in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. It is an active forager of montane cloud forests.

children.name: Myioborus pariae
children.description: The Paria Redstart has a grey head, yellow underparts, and olive upperparts, endemic to the Paria Peninsula of northeastern Venezuela. It inhabits montane cloud forests and is classified as Endangered due to its extremely restricted range.

children.name: Basileuterus
children.description: A genus of small, active warblers with bold head patterns, predominantly found in Central and South America. They are known for their bright colouration, including yellow, olive, and chestnut, often with striking crown stripes and supercilia.

children.name: Basileuterus culicivorus
children.description: The Golden-crowned Warbler has a grey head with a yellow crown stripe, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts, ranging from Mexico to Argentina. It is a common inhabitant of forests and woodlands, often joining mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Basileuterus trifasciatus
children.description: The Three-banded Warbler has a grey head with white stripes, olive upperparts, and a yellow belly, found in the Andes of southern Ecuador and Peru. It inhabits the undergrowth of montane cloud forests.

children.name: Basileuterus tristriatus
children.description: The Three-striped Warbler has a grey head with three bold black stripes, olive upperparts, and pale yellow underparts, found in the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia. It is a skulking inhabitant of montane forest undergrowth.

children.name: Basileuterus melanotis
children.description: The Black-eared Warbler has a grey head with a black ear patch, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama. It forages in the understorey of montane forests.

children.name: Basileuterus tacarcunae
children.description: The Tacarcuna Warbler has a grey head with a yellow crown stripe, olive-green upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the Darien highlands of eastern Panama and adjacent Colombia. It inhabits montane cloud forests.

children.name: Basileuterus cinereicollis
children.description: The Grey-throated Warbler has a grey throat and head, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the montane forests of Colombia and Venezuela. It forages in the understorey, often in pairs.

children.name: Basileuterus griseiceps
children.description: The Grey-headed Warbler has a grey head, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts, endemic to the coastal mountains of northern Venezuela. It is classified as Endangered due to habitat loss within its restricted range.

children.name: Basileuterus rufifrons
children.description: The Rufous-capped Warbler has a bright rufous cap, a black stripe through the eye, white supercilium, and yellow underparts, found from northern Mexico to El Salvador. It inhabits scrubby hillsides and forest edges, often skulking in dense undergrowth.

children.name: Basileuterus melanogenys
children.description: The Black-cheeked Warbler has a black cheek patch, white supercilium, grey head, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama. It is a skulking inhabitant of cloud forest understorey.

children.name: Basileuterus ignotus
children.description: The Pirre Warbler has a grey head, white supercilium, olive upperparts, and pale yellow underparts, endemic to Cerro Pirre in the Darien region of eastern Panama. It inhabits montane cloud forests and is classified as Vulnerable due to its restricted range.

children.name: Basileuterus basilicus
children.description: The Santa Marta Warbler has a grey head with a white supercilium, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, endemic to the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta in Colombia. It inhabits montane cloud forests and is classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Basileuterus chrysogaster
children.description: The Golden-bellied Warbler has a grey head, white supercilium, olive upperparts, and bright golden-yellow underparts, found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia. It inhabits humid montane forests and forages in the undergrowth.

children.name: Basileuterus bivittatus
children.description: The Two-banded Warbler has a grey head with two white stripes, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia. It inhabits the understorey of humid montane forests.

children.name: Basileuterus cabanisi
children.description: Cabanis's Warbler has a grey head, white supercilium, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the Andes from Peru to Bolivia. It inhabits the understorey of montane forests, often joining mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Basileuterus conspicillatus
children.description: The White-lored Warbler has a grey head with a white loral spot, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, endemic to the Santa Marta Mountains of Colombia. It inhabits montane cloud forests and is classified as Endangered.

children.name: Basileuterus signatus
children.description: The Pale-legged Warbler has a grey head with a white supercilium, olive upperparts, and pale yellow underparts, found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia. It inhabits the undergrowth of humid montane forests.

children.name: Myiothlypis
children.description: A genus of small warblers formerly included in Basileuterus, distinguished by their ground-foraging habits and often duller plumage. They are widespread in Central and South America, inhabiting forest understorey and edges.

children.name: Myiothlypis fulvicauda
children.description: The Buff-rumped Warbler has olive-brown upperparts, a buff-coloured rump and tail base, and pale underparts, found from Honduras to Bolivia. It is a terrestrial warbler that forages along stream banks and wet forest edges, constantly bobbing its tail.

children.name: Myiothlypis leucoblephara
children.description: The White-browed Warbler has a prominent white supercilium, olive-brown upperparts, and whitish underparts, found in southeastern Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. It inhabits the understorey of Atlantic forests and plantations.

children.name: Myiothlypis rivularis
children.description: The Riverbank Warbler has olive-brown upperparts, a white throat, and pale grey underparts, found in the lowland forests of South America east of the Andes. It forages along forest streams and rivers, bobbing its tail constantly.

children.name: Myiothlypis bivittata
children.description: The Two-banded Warbler has a grey head with two white stripes, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the Andes from Peru to Bolivia. It inhabits humid montane forests and forest edges.

children.name: Myiothlypis signata
children.description: The Pale-legged Warbler has a grey head, white supercilium, olive upperparts, and yellowish underparts with pale legs, found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia. It inhabits the undergrowth of humid montane forests.

children.name: Myiothlypis nigrocristata
children.description: The Black-crested Warbler has a black crown stripe, grey head, olive upperparts, and bright yellow underparts, found in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. It inhabits the understorey of montane cloud forests.

children.name: Myiothlypis luteoviridis
children.description: The Citrine Warbler has olive-yellow upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a distinctive yellow supercilium, found in the high Andes from Colombia to Bolivia. It inhabits elfin forests and scrub above the treeline.

children.name: Myiothlypis coronata
children.description: The Russet-crowned Warbler has a chestnut crown, grey head, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia. It inhabits the understorey of montane forests and cloud forests.

children.name: Myiothlypis flaveola
children.description: The Flavescent Warbler has olive-green upperparts, a yellow throat and breast, and a pale yellow belly, found in a restricted area of northeastern Venezuela and Trinidad. It inhabits dry forests and scrub.

children.name: Myiothlypis blanchardes
children.description: Blanchard's Warbler has a grey head, white supercilium, olive upperparts, and pale underparts, found in the Paria Peninsula of Venezuela. It inhabits montane cloud forests and is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Myiothlypis mesoleuca
children.description: The White-bellied Warbler has olive-brown upperparts, a white belly, and a prominent white supercilium, found in the lowland forests of the Guiana Shield region.

children.name: Myiothlypis albifacies
children.description: The White-faced Warbler has a white face with a black crown, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts, found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Oreothlypis
children.description: A small genus of brightly coloured warblers with short tails and relatively large bills, found in highland forests from Mexico to Panama. They are active foragers that often join mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Oreothlypis superciliosa
children.description: The Crescent-chested Warbler has a grey head with a white crescent below the eye, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts with a chestnut crescent on the breast. It inhabits highland forests from Mexico to Nicaragua.

children.name: Oreothlypis gutturalis
children.description: The Flame-throated Warbler has a brilliant orange-red throat, grey head and upperparts, and a white belly, found in the highlands of Costa Rica and Panama. It inhabits montane oak forests and forages actively in the canopy.

children.name: Helmitheros
children.description: The worm-eating warbler — a monotypic genus of relatively large, terrestrial warbler named for its habit of eating caterpillars and other larvae. It has a distinctive striped head pattern and forages on the ground among dead leaves.

children.name: Helmitheros vermivorum
children.description: A plain olive-brown warbler with a distinctive head pattern of black stripes on a buffy background, and a relatively long, slender bill. It forages on the ground, flipping dead leaves in search of caterpillars and other insects. It breeds in mature deciduous forests of the eastern United States and winters in the Caribbean and Central America.

children.name: Limnothlypis
children.description: A monotypic genus of large, secretive warbler named for its swamp habitat. It has a long bill and a distinctive habit of foraging among dead leaves and debris on the forest floor.

children.name: Limnothlypis swainsonii
children.description: A large, plain brown warbler with a long, slender bill and a prominent white supercilium, named for its habit of foraging among dead leaves in swamp forests of the southeastern United States. It is secretive and more often heard than seen.

children.name: Euthlypis
children.description: The fan-tailed warbler — a monotypic genus of striking warbler with a long, constantly fanned tail. It is a skulking bird of forest understorey in Central America and Mexico.

children.name: Euthlypis lachrymosa
children.description: The Fan-tailed Warbler is a striking yellow-and-black warbler with a long, constantly fanned tail, found from northern Mexico to Nicaragua. It inhabits the understorey of dry forests and scrub, skulking in dense vegetation.
